design_philosophy.core_loop: Select one of three characters, fight through horizontally-scrolling stages of enemy waves, break interactive environmental objects for health/items, reach and defeat boss at stage end, progress to next location
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing health between checkpoints while balancing offense against numerically superior enemy waves; choosing when to spend Special Moves versus conserve resources
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as a martial arts master or street fighter taking down a criminal organization across international locations; cooperative heroism with a friend
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Character-focused design philosophy—each of the three playable characters (Haggar, Maki, Carlos) has distinctly different movesets and playstyles, forcing player to commit to a fighting style rather than offering universal tools. The game also enforces narrative progression through fixed geographic locations (Hong Kong → France → Holland → England → Italy → Japan) rather than allowing player choice in route

difficulty.lives_system: Extra lives obtainable through 'Guy Doll' items found in breakable objects; death returns player to checkpoint
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based checkpointing; each of six stages constitutes a checkpoint with boss encounter at conclusion
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Adjustable difficulty setting in options menu directly impacts game progression and narrative; full ending only visible on Expert difficulty setting, similar to Final Fight Guy. Lower difficulties reveal partial endings

progression.upgrade_system: No persistent character upgrades between stages. Equipment (tonfa, lumber, knife) and power-ups (Genryusai Doll for invulnerability, Guy Doll for extra life) are found within stages as consumable resources
progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear stage progression; each stage must be completed in sequence to advance to next geographic location. No branching paths or optional stages documented
replayability_hooks: Three distinct playable characters with unique movesets encourage replays with different fighting styles
Two-player cooperative mode adds social replayability dimension
Difficulty-gated narrative content (full ending only on Expert) incentivizes replaying on harder settings
Code allows two players to select same character, enabling different co-op strategies

world_building_techniques: Geographic progression narrative: six distinct Eurasian locations (Hong Kong, France, Holland, England, Italy, Japan) serve as visual and thematic progression
Character-driven plot: rescue mission for Maki's sister Rena and father Genryusai connects to first game's protagonist Guy
Enemy roster variation: distinct enemy types per region suggest local criminal organization presence
Time stamps on each stage (12:00pm Hong Kong through 11:30pm Japan) suggest real-time chase sequence across globe
Boss names and designs reflect geographic locations (Won Won in Hong Kong, Freddie in France, Retu as Japan final boss)

boss_design.philosophy: Stage-culminating encounters with unique named antagonists representing regional Mad Gear leadership; each boss appears designed to test the player's mastery of their chosen character's toolkit against a single powerful opponent
notable_bosses: Won Won (Hong Kong) - wields meat cleaver in Japanese version, removed in international versions
Rolento (Italy) - returning boss from original Final Fight, omitted from first SNES port, restored here
Retu (Japan) - final boss and new leader of revived Mad Gear organization

faction_indicators: Mad Gear Gang resurgence across multiple nations suggests transnational criminal network
Regional boss hierarchy (Won Won, Freddie, Bratken, Philippe, Rolento, Retu) implies distributed leadership structure
Enemy character variations by region (Mary/Eliza knife-wielders in Japan version, Leon/Robert in international versions) suggest localized recruitment or skill sets
danger_signals: Kidnapping of Rena and Genryusai indicates willingness to use hostages as leverage against heroes
Multi-nation operation shows organizational scale and reach
Coordinated revenge against original Final Fight trio suggests institutional memory and grudge maintenance
environmental_storytelling: Breakable barrels and drums containing health/items suggest civilian supply lines or street-level economy
Multiple weapon types (tonfa, lumber, knife) scattered throughout stages imply availability of improvised combat tools
Bonus stages (Break Car, Break Drum) suggest destructible infrastructure as collateral in gang conflicts
Six distinct international settings with unique visual theming establish Mad Gear as global rather than localized threat
